---
title: "Discussion and Conclusion"
---

## Discusssion and Conclusion

The gap between the theoretical mode (117.1) and the simulated mean (122.2) is expected — the distribution of the maximum is right-skewed, so the mean is pulled above the mode by the long upper tail. The mode remains the most appropriate summary statistic here because it represents the most *likely* maximum defensive rating among contenders, rather than the average, which is inflated by rare cases of deep tournament runs by teams with weaker defenses.

The simulation confirms that the shifted exponential model is a reasonable fit for this data: the theoretical PDF closely tracks the simulated histogram, and the simulated median of 120.3 sits near the mode, consistent with the moderate right skew expected from an order statistic of this form.

Based on the simulation, the median value of 120.3 is the maximum possible ADR a team could have if they want to have a realistic shot at the national championship. A team with this rating is extremely unlikely to actually win however, as this value estimates an absolute maximum. A much lower value (a value within the 90s) would more likely correlate with postseason success.

Our research backs up the importance of a good defensive system in order for a team to have justifiable aspirations for a deep postseason run. Historically, defensive ratings have been an above-average indicator for championship-caliber teams, and the mantra "defense wins championships" has stood the test of time for a reason.

## Limitations

Future research could analyze other elements of the game, such as adjusted offensive rating, shooting percentage, or differences in performance based on home court versus away games. ADR, while useful as its own metric, doesn't show the full picture in terms of a team's success. Different teams can have different tactics that they use that focuses on different elements of the game.
